    "id": "w-mnszc0r5-0b0bbb797412",
    "type": "quest",
    "status": "open",
    "title": "Explicit contracts principle — make implicit shared understanding into upfront artifacts",
    "body": "<!-- Live body for this quest is a file in the vibers guild. See .claude/skills/quests/SKILL.md. -->\n<!-- Do not edit this row body while the quest is live. -->\n\n## Goal\n\nThe principle: when LLM agents share implicit understanding — with each other, or with a human — they fill the gap with their own inferences, and those inferences diverge in model-specific ways that don't surface until integration or review. The fix is structural: write the shared understanding down as an artifact, upfront, before execution begins. The artifact is the \"contract.\" This quest is the principle's home. It names the pattern, enumerates the known flavors, and serves as the anchor for application quests via `instance_of` links.",
    "createdAt": "2026-04-10T14:06:44.177Z",
    "updatedAt": "2026-04-13T21:36:06.958Z"
  },
  {
    "id": "w-mnsz2ku6-8e973193e957",
    "type": "quest",
    "status": "open",
    "title": "Work-tracking primitives beyond writs+quests — backlog, seeds, threads",
    "body": "<!-- Live body for this quest is a file in the vibers guild. See .claude/skills/quests/SKILL.md. -->\n<!-- Do not edit this row body while the quest is live. -->\n\n## Goal\n\nDecide whether Nexus needs additional work-tracking primitives beyond the current writ+quest substrate — specifically, whether some of the shapes GSD distinguishes (backlog items, seeds with trigger conditions, persistent context threads) warrant their own writ types, or whether they can be expressed as subtypes/status conventions on existing primitives. The outcome is (a) a taxonomy of the \"parked work\" shapes we actually need to distinguish, (b) a decision on which of those shapes get their own writ type vs. which are conventions on existing types, (c) for any new types, a sketch of the type-specific fields and the lifecycle, and (d) particularly for the \"seeds\" pattern — a concrete design for semantic trigger conditions that surface parked writs automatically when a matching event occurs.",
    "createdAt": "2026-04-10T13:59:23.646Z",
    "updatedAt": "2026-04-13T21:36:06.957Z"
  },
  {
    "id": "w-mnsz1s1x-0fe2689d16c0",
    "type": "quest",
    "status": "open",
    "title": "Explicit design contracts as first-class planning artifacts (beyond UI)",
    "body": "<!-- Live body for this quest is a file in the vibers guild. See .claude/skills/quests/SKILL.md. -->\n<!-- Do not edit this row body while the quest is live. -->\n\n## Goal\n\nDecide whether Nexus should treat explicit design contracts — structured artifacts that pin down spacing/typography/color/copy for UI work, naming/layout/error-handling for code work, vocabulary/register for writing work — as a first-class category of planning artifact, produced before execution and enforced during review. The outcome is (a) a clear articulation of the \"contract\" concept generalized beyond UI (GSD's framing is UI-specific but the principle isn't), (b) an inventory of the contract surfaces Nexus work touches (UI, code style, domain vocabulary, commit message style, commission-review rubrics), (c) a recommendation on which surfaces actually benefit from explicit contracts vs. which are better left implicit, and (d) a sketch of where contracts would live (a new writ type? a field on mandate writs? a companion doc in the codex?).",
    "createdAt": "2026-04-10T13:58:46.341Z",
    "updatedAt": "2026-04-13T21:36:06.957Z"
  },

    "id": "w-mnsz0ve6-56c21ebc62bb",
    "type": "quest",
    "status": "open",
    "title": "Assumptions mode — invert the discussion default from 'ask me' to 'correct me'",
    "body": "<!-- Live body for this quest is a file in the vibers guild. See .claude/skills/quests/SKILL.md. -->\n<!-- Do not edit this row body while the quest is live. -->\n\n## Goal\n\nAdopt, for Nexus agents that conduct design conversations with Sean (primarily Coco, secondarily Astrolabe's analyst stage), an \"assumptions mode\" as an interaction default — where the agent reads the relevant code/docs/context first, drafts a structured set of assumptions about what Sean wants and how it would approach the work, and asks Sean to confirm, correct, or expand — instead of asking open-ended clarifying questions. The outcome is (a) a clear articulation of when assumptions mode is better than open-ended mode and when it isn't, (b) a sketch of what \"read the codebase first\" looks like in practice for Coco (which tools, how much reading is too much), (c) a lightweight behavior change captured in Coco's agent instructions, and (d) an assessment of whether the same pattern should be built into the Astrolabe analyst stage.",

=== w-mnt3t5h8-943e2a2ef85f ===
# Astrolabe efficiency: reader/full-inventory cost reduction

## Goal

Cut Astrolabe's end-to-end latency and token cost substantially, with the initial focus on the reader stage and its full-repository inventory pass — currently suspected to be the dominant contributor. The outcome is (a) a profile of where Astrolabe's time and tokens actually go across its stages, (b) a chosen set of interventions on the reader/inventory path (scoping, caching, incremental re-reads, hierarchical summarization, or similar), and (c) a sketch of any cross-stage changes that follow from the profiling (e.g., handoff format changes, dropping work the reader does that downstream stages don't use).

## Status

active — **all 6 rigs landed at `decision-review` 2026-04-14**; first-pass cost/timing analysis done; SSR hypothesis looks broken (not achieving predicted 10–20× reader reduction); MRA looks like the winner on cost and wall-clock; quality comparison still pending. Tool-dropout failure on baseline/MCP-precond recurred a second time, was recovered via DB edit, and spun out as its own quest (`w-mnyp279g-f565be10b053`).

## Current batch (re-dispatched 2026-04-14)

| Variant | Brief | Writ | Rig | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| baseline (`brief`) | MCP-preconditions | `w-mnynxpwd-17535f30f61a` | `rig-mnynxqnu-798d4a34` | running |
| baseline (`brief`) | Prompt-injection | `w-mnynxfhj-2b38ea890673` | `rig-mnynxidw-fad80c71` | running |
| SSR (`brief-ssr`) | MCP-preconditions | `w-mnynxz7n-d658562985a6` | `rig-mnyny2tp-5ac56846` | running |
| SSR (`brief-ssr`) | Prompt-injection | `w-mnyny4bg-28b1974bb033` | `rig-mnyny78s-f7a83b39` | running |
| MRA (`brief-mra`) | MCP-preconditions | `w-mnynycgm-2200291d4972` | `rig-mnynyfjz-b9890309` | running |
| MRA (`brief-mra`) | Prompt-injection | `w-mnynyjub-a5398e15e8ea` | *(not yet started at table time)* | running |

### Per-rig outcomes (as of 2026-04-14 mid-afternoon)

- **`rig-mnynxqnu-798d4a34`** (baseline / MCP-preconditions) — Reader session `ses-mnynxr8e-500ec54d` burned $3.57 / 14min and narrated its own failure in prose after 6 `inventory-write` tool calls all returned HTTP 500. Second recurrence of this exact failure class on this exact writ. **Recovered** via transcript extraction + direct DB state-edit (see Recovery section); analyst is now running against the restored inventory.
- **5 other rigs** — ran clean through reader and analyst stages; blocked at `decision-review` awaiting patron.
- **Spun out quest `w-mnyp279g-f565be10b053`** to chase the root cause of the baseline-reader mid-session MCP 500s. That failure mode is **distinct** from the start-time precondition failure addressed by `w-mny2ltvy` — here the tool server was reachable at session start and the engine did real work before the transport died. If we find a fix, the baseline reader stops being fragile on this class of brief; if we don't, this is another point in favor of SSR/MRA (both variants ran clean on the identical writ body).

### Recovery of rig-mnynxqnu (2026-04-14)

When the second recurrence hit, Sean authorized recovery of the failing rig rather than throwing out the cell. Coco:
=== w-mnyx6ajh-ae9d4622cf36 ===
# GSD-intel-style atlas for Astrolabe reader

## Goal

Port the GSD `/gsd-intel` pattern into Astrolabe: maintain a queryable project-shape artifact (structural + interpretive intel) that downstream planning stages consume instead of re-scanning the codebase from scratch on every commission. Outcome is (a) an intel schema tailored to what Astrolabe's reader/analyst/spec-writer actually need, (b) a build/refresh mechanism split between cheap non-LLM tooling (structural) and a one-time LLM mapper pass (interpretive), with freshness tracking, and (c) a reader/MRA prompt shape that consumes the intel as preamble or as MCP-tool-backed queries — turning the reader from a 25-turn exploration into a single-shot or near-single-shot pass over prepared context.

## Status

active — 2026-04-14. Pitch captured; GSD source dive complete (`experiments/data/2026-04-14-gsd-intel-teardown.md`); three-axes framing settled (storage shape × access shape × production shape); implementation work decomposed into three layer-subquests ordered 1 → 2 → 3. Parent-quest role is now scaffolding and cross-cutting design rather than direct implementation — each layer has its own subquest carrying the detail.

## Implementation plan — three layers, in order

The design work has consolidated around a three-layer architecture. Each layer has its own subquest. Implementation proceeds strictly in order: ship layer 1, measure it, then ship layer 2, then ship layer 3 (with its design work running in parallel with 1+2 because of multi-rig coupling).

1. **Layer 1 — Prose preamble generated by LLM mapper** → `w-mnz8kj86-141e5b12bf7c`
   - Turn-killer. Prose markdown docs built by an LLM mapper rig and injected as preamble into planning stages, selected per commission by a brief-type → document-subset map.
   - This is the bet that directly addresses the SSR failure: single-shot fails without prep, so we build the prep once and amortize it.
   - Ships first because it's the highest-leverage single change and proves or disproves the core hypothesis (prep-then-single-shot) cheaply.
2. **Layer 2 — Deterministic repository facts** → `w-mnz8kqb9-266e6bb9f95c`
   - Precision layer. Structured data built by non-LLM tooling (tree-sitter, custom walkers over framework metadata, git plumbing) — symbol index, import graph, test↔source map, public-API surfaces, churn data.
   - Consumed two ways: (a) cheap MCP tools for on-demand fact-checks, (b) structured snippets injected into layer 1's preamble when a commission needs exact-name precision alongside the prose.
   - Ships second because its value is bounded without layer 1 and its cost story depends on piggy-backing on layer 1's preamble-cache story.
3. **Layer 3 — Combatting staleness** → `w-mnz8kxki-bbe3be09d408`
   - Freshness and refresh scheduling. Git-SHA-based drift detection, per-layer refresh policies (cheap for layer 2, expensive for layer 1), and — crucially — the design where atlas freshness becomes a **declared engine precondition** that the multi-rig scheduler satisfies by auto-prepending a mapper rig.
   - Ships third (layers 1 and 2 have to exist to be worth keeping fresh) but *design* work starts early and in parallel because it couples tightly into the multi-rig quest cluster (`w-mnsxawnd`, `w-mnsxbn8m`) — decisions landing there could foreclose layer 3's options.

## Next Steps

1. **Await parent-quest A/B quality comparison.** Layer 1's consumer is conditional on whether MRA or baseline wins on quality (`w-mnt3t5h8` gate). Layer shape doesn't change; only the rig-template wiring does.
2. **Layer 1 kickoff** once the gate clears. Settle the document set, draft the brief-type → subset map, decide on the retrieval-layer question (BM25 vs vector vs static map only; see layer 1's open questions), spike one mapper rig end-to-end.
=== w-mnz8kj86-141e5b12bf7c ===
# Atlas layer 1: prose preamble generated by LLM mapper

## Goal

Design and ship the first layer of the Astrolabe atlas: a set of prose markdown documents produced by an LLM mapper rig and injected as preamble into Astrolabe's planning stages, selected per commission by a brief-type → document-subset map. This is the "turn-killer" layer that directly addresses the SSR failure mode (single-shot without prep doesn't work; single-shot *with* prebuilt interpretive context does). Outcome is (a) a settled document set (which of GSD's seven templates port, what we add), (b) a brief-type → subset map Astrolabe uses to pick 2–3 docs per commission, (c) a mapper rig template that produces the documents, and (d) the reader/MRA prompt changes that consume them.

## Status

open — just opened 2026-04-14 as the first of three implementation-layer subquests under `w-mnyx6ajh`. This is the *first layer to ship* per parent-quest ordering. Blocked on the parent quest's A/B quality-comparison gate: if MRA wins quality, this layer feeds MRA; if baseline wins, it feeds the current reader stage. Either way the layer shape is the same — the consumer just changes.

## Next Steps

1. **Settle the document set.** Starting from GSD's seven mapper templates, pick which port cleanly, which get rewritten for our context, and what's missing. Current gut:
   - **Keep**: STACK (our framework stack, published/dev modes, guild plugins), ARCHITECTURE (books/engines/standing orders/writ types), STRUCTURE (monorepo layout, sanctum vs vibers vs framework, where-to-add guidance), CONVENTIONS (TypeScript style, test conventions, commit conventions, the self-document-for-agents rule).
   - **Drop**: INTEGRATIONS (GSD's shape assumes SaaS integrations, not our shape), CONCERNS (redundant with the quest system and commission-log).
   - **Unsure**: TESTING (our test story is framework-heavy and may be worth a standalone doc; could also fold into CONVENTIONS).
   - **Add**: MODULE-MAP (per-package: public API surface, role in the system, dependents and dependencies, churn hotspots). This is the document the reader currently works hardest to reconstruct from filesystem grepping.
2. **Design the brief-type → subset map.** Enumerate the brief shapes Astrolabe actually sees and pick 2–3 docs per shape. First-pass categories to iterate on:
   - *framework-internal* (packages/framework/...) → ARCHITECTURE + MODULE-MAP + CONVENTIONS
   - *plugin work* (packages/plugins/...) → MODULE-MAP + CONVENTIONS + ARCHITECTURE
   - *engine work* (plugin engines + rig templates) → MODULE-MAP + ARCHITECTURE + whatever "how engines are structured" ends up as
   - *CLI / nsg work* → MODULE-MAP + STRUCTURE + CONVENTIONS
   - *sanctum-side work* → STRUCTURE + CONVENTIONS (probably different CONVENTIONS doc than framework)
   - *doc-only commissions* → STRUCTURE only
   - Validate by walking the last N commissions and checking whether the predicted subset would have been *sufficient* for planning.
3. **Draft the mapper rig template.** A standalone Astrolabe-adjacent rig that runs the mapping work. Open design questions:
   - Single orchestrator + parallel mapper-agent fan-out (GSD style, 4 agents writing 7 docs)?
   - Or one sequential pass (cheaper to reason about, slower in wall-clock)?
   - Where does the output land — sanctum (`experiments/atlas/<workspace-name>/`), guild-side book, or worktree-adjacent?
   - What's the refresh trigger mechanism — a commission kind, a standing order, or manual `nsg atlas refresh`? (Staleness quest `w-mnz8kxki` owns the scheduling question; layer 1 just needs to produce when invoked.)
=== w-mnz8kqb9-266e6bb9f95c ===
# Atlas layer 2: deterministic repository facts

## Goal

Design and ship the second layer of the Astrolabe atlas: deterministically computed structured facts about the repository (symbol index, dependency graph, test↔source map, public-API surface per package, recent-churn data) produced by non-LLM tooling. These facts back two consumption paths — (a) cheap MCP tools Astrolabe agents can call to verify ground truth mid-session without triggering a filesystem grind, and (b) selective structured snippets injected alongside the prose preamble when a commission specifically benefits from exact-name precision (e.g. a refactor commission that needs the real list of call sites, not an LLM's paraphrase). Outcome is (a) a list of fact types and their shapes, (b) tool selections (tree-sitter? madge? ast-grep? custom?), (c) a query surface definition, and (d) a decision on prose-alongside-structured preamble injection.

## Status

open — just opened 2026-04-14 as the second of three implementation-layer subquests under `w-mnyx6ajh`. Sequenced *after* layer 1 (`w-mnz8kj86`) per parent-quest ordering — layer 1 ships the turn-killer preamble; layer 2 adds the precision/fact-check layer on top. Can start design work in parallel with layer 1 implementation, but shouldn't ship until layer 1 has proven that preamble-shaped consumption actually moves the cost needle.

## Next Steps

1. **Enumerate the fact types we need.** Start from the questions Astrolabe's reader currently burns turns answering and back out what structured data would short-circuit each one. Seed list to iterate on:
   - **Symbol index**: "does this function exist, in which file, with what signature?"
   - **Exports per file**: "what's the public API of this module?"
   - **Import graph**: "who depends on this file? what does this file depend on?"
   - **Test↔source map**: "which tests cover this source file?"
   - **Package public-API surface**: "what does this package expose to consumers, not just internally?"
   - **Recent churn**: "which files have changed in the last N commits / are commonly modified together?"
   - **File-tree metadata**: "what's the shape of this directory, how big are the files, what are the types?"
   - **Build/config fingerprint**: "what's the tsconfig, lint config, test runner, formatter?"
2. **Pick query shapes per fact type.** For each fact, what question does a consumer ask?
   - Exact lookup (`atlas-exports <file>` → list of symbols)
   - Reverse lookup (`atlas-importers <file>` → list of files importing it)
   - Filtered traversal (`atlas-deps <package> --transitive` → dep closure)
   - Fuzzy/prefix search (`atlas-find-symbol <pattern>` → ranked matches)
   - Structural aggregation (`atlas-package-api <package>` → exported surface)
   - Differential (`atlas-churn --since <sha>` → changed files list)
3. **Select tooling per fact type.** This is where the real decisions live. Candidates to evaluate:
   - **tree-sitter** — cross-language parser, strong for symbol extraction and AST walks. Good match for the framework (TypeScript) and plugin code. Well-maintained grammars.
=== w-mnz8kxki-bbe3be09d408 ===
# Atlas layer 3: combatting staleness

## Goal

Decide and ship the staleness model for the Astrolabe atlas: how we detect drift, when we trigger refreshes, and how expensive refresh costs get amortized or avoided. Covers incremental structural-fact updates on file change, periodic full prose re-maps, git-SHA-based drift detection, and — most importantly — the design where "a fresh atlas map for workspace X" becomes a **declared engine precondition** that the multi-rig scheduler can satisfy by auto-prepending a mapper rig before the planning rig runs. This is where the atlas stops being a manually-maintained artifact and becomes a scheduled dependency of planning work. Outcome is (a) a drift-detection strategy (what counts as "stale enough to matter"), (b) a refresh policy per atlas layer (prose layer and deterministic layer refresh on different cadences), (c) a design for engine-declared atlas preconditions that couples into the multi-rig template-generation work, and (d) a clear story for how this interacts with the workspace model.

## Status

open — just opened 2026-04-14 as the third of three implementation-layer subquests under `w-mnyx6ajh`. Sequenced *last* per parent-quest ordering: staleness is only a problem once there's a valuable artifact to keep fresh, which requires layers 1 and 2 to have shipped and proven value. But the design work can start in parallel — especially the "engine-declared precondition" piece, because it couples tightly into the multi-rig refactor quest cluster and may influence decisions that get made there.

## Next Steps

1. **Pick a drift-detection primitive.** The easy wins first:
   - **Git SHA tracking**: store the SHA the atlas was built against; staleness = "any file in scope has changed since that SHA." Cheap (`git diff --name-only <sha>..HEAD`), correct, no false positives from wall-clock.
   - **File content hash**: per-file content hash stored alongside each layer-2 fact; invalidate on hash mismatch. More granular than SHA tracking, more metadata to maintain.
   - **Wall-clock TTL**: GSD's approach. Bad — false-positive on overnight, false-negative on active edits. Document as "don't do this."
   - **Change-coverage ratio**: "atlas is stale if >N% of in-scope files have changed since build." Tunable, useful as a refresh-*trigger* heuristic on top of exact SHA/hash tracking.
   Decide which combinations we need. My lean: SHA tracking as the base primitive, file-hash for per-fact invalidation in layer 2, change-coverage as the refresh trigger threshold.
2. **Define refresh policies per layer.** Layer 1 and layer 2 have different refresh cost profiles and should refresh on different cadences:
   - **Layer 2 (deterministic)**: near-zero cost per refresh. Can rebuild on every commit, every file save, every commission kickoff, whatever's convenient. The expensive question is *where* to trigger it (file watcher? git post-commit hook? engine precondition? commissioned rig?) not *whether* it's affordable.
   - **Layer 1 (prose)**: expensive per refresh ($5–10 for a full mapping run at GSD scale). Needs amortization across many commissions. Refresh only when interpretive drift is likely — not just "a file changed" but "enough files changed, or architecturally-important files changed, that the narrative is probably out of date." This is where change-coverage thresholds and possibly file-importance weights come in.
3. **Design the engine-precondition mechanism.** This is the load-bearing piece. Sketch:
   - An Astrolabe planning engine declares a *dependency* on "a fresh atlas for workspace X."
   - The scheduler checks atlas freshness before dispatching the planning engine.
   - If fresh: proceed.
   - If stale: the scheduler auto-prepends a mapper rig (or a smaller refresh rig for incremental updates) to the writ's rig sequence. The planning engine waits for the mapper to complete.
   - Atlas freshness is re-checked after the mapper completes; the planning engine then dispatches against the now-fresh atlas.
   - This is essentially "backward chaining on precondition satisfaction," which is one of the exact shapes the multi-rig template-generation quest (`w-mnsxawnd`) is already considering.
4. **Couple into the multi-rig quest cluster.** Read and engage with:
   - `w-mnsxawnd-5f868b6491d9` — *Rig template generation beyond 'one template per writ type'* — this is where the "engine declares a precondition, scheduler synthesizes a rig shape that satisfies it" primitive lives or should live. Atlas-freshness-as-precondition is a concrete use case that can inform that quest's design.
=== w-mnyp279g-f565be10b053 ===
# Tool-server MCP 500s drop mid-session on baseline astrolabe reader

## Goal

Find the root cause of a recurring failure mode in which a baseline astrolabe reader session starts with the MCP tool server healthy, successfully makes multiple MCP tool calls, and then mid-session every `inventory-write` (and other astrolabe tool) call starts returning HTTP 500 for the remainder of the session. The session's last-ditch retry loop burns several dollars and exits 0 without a usable inventory. We want to know *why the tool server stops responding mid-session*, not just defend against the symptom — this is distinct from the engine-precondition quest (`w-mny2ltvy`), which addresses the case where the tool is never reachable at start.

## Status

active — opened 2026-04-14 after the *second* recurrence on the same writ. Two sample rigs captured as evidence. Hypotheses are explicitly untested; this is the investigation bookmark.

## Next Steps

1. **Stand up a side-by-side transcript diff** of the two failed reader sessions. Look at tool-call history ordered by timestamp — when does the first 500 appear, what tool, what was the last successful call before it, how many tools had been called, what was the cumulative byte volume of tool *arguments* and *results*.
2. **Check the guild daemon log** (`/workspace/vibers/.nexus/logs/`) for anything interesting around the failure timestamps: `2026-04-14T13:40-13:48` (rig-mnynxqnu session `ses-mnynxr8e`) and the earlier `rig-mny49p49` window. Look specifically for SSE disconnects, tool-server restarts, Node unhandled rejections, EMFILE/ECONNRESET, proxy errors from the babysitter.
3. **Inspect the tool-server → babysitter transport.** The babysitter runs `createProxyMcpHttpServer()` (claude-code/src/babysitter.ts ~L238-353) which speaks SSE and forwards to `guildToolUrl`. Is the SSE channel dying (from either side) after N requests or T seconds? Does the proxy try to reconnect? Does a dead SSE channel return 500 to the model while the tool server is actually fine?
4. **Reproduce.** Pick the baseline reader template, give it the MCP-preconditions brief again, and run it in isolation under logging. If it reproduces reliably → bisect.
5. **Decide whether to add defenses** (retry on 500, proxy reconnect, tool-server client pool sizing) vs fixing the root cause alone.

## Context

### The failure shape

A session runs the astrolabe reader engine. Early in the session, MCP tool calls succeed — `plan-show`, possibly one or more `inventory-write` calls. Then, at some point partway through, *every* subsequent MCP tool call returns HTTP 500. The session's model-side retry logic retries repeatedly, burning tokens and wall-clock, and eventually gives up and narrates its own failure in prose. The session process exits 0 (no crash, no thrown exception) but the plan row has no inventory written, so the downstream `inventory-check` engine fails and the rig cascades to `failed`.

Crucially: **the early successful tool calls prove the tool server was reachable at start.** This is not a startup/precondition failure — the transport came up cleanly, did real work, and *then* died.

### Sample rigs

Both failures happened on the same writ (`w-mnynxpwd-17535f30f61a` — `[EXP] Engine-level MCP tool precondition checks`), baseline (`brief`) variant, in the astrolabe reader stage. The writ body is notably "meta" — a brief *about* MCP tool failures, citing a prior incident.

# GSD-intel-style atlas for Astrolabe reader

## Goal

Port the GSD `/gsd-intel` pattern into Astrolabe: maintain a queryable project-shape artifact (structural + interpretive intel) that downstream planning stages consume instead of re-scanning the codebase from scratch on every commission. Outcome is (a) an intel schema tailored to what Astrolabe's reader/analyst/spec-writer actually need, (b) a build/refresh mechanism split between cheap non-LLM tooling (structural) and a one-time LLM mapper pass (interpretive), with freshness tracking, and (c) a reader/MRA prompt shape that consumes the intel as preamble or as MCP-tool-backed queries — turning the reader from a 25-turn exploration into a single-shot or near-single-shot pass over prepared context.

## Status

active — 2026-04-14. Pitch captured; GSD source dive complete (`experiments/data/2026-04-14-gsd-intel-teardown.md`); three-axes framing settled (storage shape × access shape × production shape); implementation work decomposed into three layer-subquests ordered 1 → 2 → 3. Parent-quest role is now scaffolding and cross-cutting design rather than direct implementation — each layer has its own subquest carrying the detail.

## Implementation plan — three layers, in order

The design work has consolidated around a three-layer architecture. Each layer has its own subquest. Implementation proceeds strictly in order: ship layer 1, measure it, then ship layer 2, then ship layer 3 (with its design work running in parallel with 1+2 because of multi-rig coupling).

1. **Layer 1 — Prose preamble generated by LLM mapper** → `w-mnz8kj86-141e5b12bf7c`
   - Turn-killer. Prose markdown docs built by an LLM mapper rig and injected as preamble into planning stages, selected per commission by a brief-type → document-subset map.
   - This is the bet that directly addresses the SSR failure: single-shot fails without prep, so we build the prep once and amortize it.
   - Ships first because it's the highest-leverage single change and proves or disproves the core hypothesis (prep-then-single-shot) cheaply.
2. **Layer 2 — Deterministic repository facts** → `w-mnz8kqb9-266e6bb9f95c`
   - Precision layer. Structured data built by non-LLM tooling (tree-sitter, custom walkers over framework metadata, git plumbing) — symbol index, import graph, test↔source map, public-API surfaces, churn data.
   - Consumed two ways: (a) cheap MCP tools for on-demand fact-checks, (b) structured snippets injected into layer 1's preamble when a commission needs exact-name precision alongside the prose.
   - Ships second because its value is bounded without layer 1 and its cost story depends on piggy-backing on layer 1's preamble-cache story.
3. **Layer 3 — Combatting staleness** → `w-mnz8kxki-bbe3be09d408`
   - Freshness and refresh scheduling. Git-SHA-based drift detection, per-layer refresh policies (cheap for layer 2, expensive for layer 1), and — crucially — the design where atlas freshness becomes a **declared engine precondition** that the multi-rig scheduler satisfies by auto-prepending a mapper rig.
   - Ships third (layers 1 and 2 have to exist to be worth keeping fresh) but *design* work starts early and in parallel because it couples tightly into the multi-rig quest cluster (`w-mnsxawnd`, `w-mnsxbn8m`) — decisions landing there could foreclose layer 3's options.

## Next Steps

1. **Await parent-quest A/B quality comparison.** Layer 1's consumer is conditional on whether MRA or baseline wins on quality (`w-mnt3t5h8` gate). Layer shape doesn't change; only the rig-template wiring does.
2. **Layer 1 kickoff** once the gate clears. Settle the document set, draft the brief-type → subset map, decide on the retrieval-layer question (BM25 vs vector vs static map only; see layer 1's open questions), spike one mapper rig end-to-end.

# Atlas layer 1: prose preamble generated by LLM mapper

## Goal

Design and ship the first layer of the Astrolabe atlas: a set of prose markdown documents produced by an LLM mapper rig and injected as preamble into Astrolabe's planning stages, selected per commission by a brief-type → document-subset map. This is the "turn-killer" layer that directly addresses the SSR failure mode (single-shot without prep doesn't work; single-shot *with* prebuilt interpretive context does). Outcome is (a) a settled document set (which of GSD's seven templates port, what we add), (b) a brief-type → subset map Astrolabe uses to pick 2–3 docs per commission, (c) a mapper rig template that produces the documents, and (d) the reader/MRA prompt changes that consume them.

## Status

open — just opened 2026-04-14 as the first of three implementation-layer subquests under `w-mnyx6ajh`. This is the *first layer to ship* per parent-quest ordering. Blocked on the parent quest's A/B quality-comparison gate: if MRA wins quality, this layer feeds MRA; if baseline wins, it feeds the current reader stage. Either way the layer shape is the same — the consumer just changes.

## Next Steps

1. **Settle the document set.** Starting from GSD's seven mapper templates, pick which port cleanly, which get rewritten for our context, and what's missing. Current gut:
   - **Keep**: STACK (our framework stack, published/dev modes, guild plugins), ARCHITECTURE (books/engines/standing orders/writ types), STRUCTURE (monorepo layout, sanctum vs vibers vs framework, where-to-add guidance), CONVENTIONS (TypeScript style, test conventions, commit conventions, the self-document-for-agents rule).
   - **Drop**: INTEGRATIONS (GSD's shape assumes SaaS integrations, not our shape), CONCERNS (redundant with the quest system and commission-log).
   - **Unsure**: TESTING (our test story is framework-heavy and may be worth a standalone doc; could also fold into CONVENTIONS).
   - **Add**: MODULE-MAP (per-package: public API surface, role in the system, dependents and dependencies, churn hotspots). This is the document the reader currently works hardest to reconstruct from filesystem grepping.
2. **Design the brief-type → subset map.** Enumerate the brief shapes Astrolabe actually sees and pick 2–3 docs per shape. First-pass categories to iterate on:
   - *framework-internal* (packages/framework/...) → ARCHITECTURE + MODULE-MAP + CONVENTIONS
   - *plugin work* (packages/plugins/...) → MODULE-MAP + CONVENTIONS + ARCHITECTURE
   - *engine work* (plugin engines + rig templates) → MODULE-MAP + ARCHITECTURE + whatever "how engines are structured" ends up as
   - *CLI / nsg work* → MODULE-MAP + STRUCTURE + CONVENTIONS
   - *sanctum-side work* → STRUCTURE + CONVENTIONS (probably different CONVENTIONS doc than framework)
   - *doc-only commissions* → STRUCTURE only
   - Validate by walking the last N commissions and checking whether the predicted subset would have been *sufficient* for planning.
3. **Draft the mapper rig template.** A standalone Astrolabe-adjacent rig that runs the mapping work. Open design questions:
   - Single orchestrator + parallel mapper-agent fan-out (GSD style, 4 agents writing 7 docs)?
   - Or one sequential pass (cheaper to reason about, slower in wall-clock)?
   - Where does the output land — sanctum (`experiments/atlas/<workspace-name>/`), guild-side book, or worktree-adjacent?
   - What's the refresh trigger mechanism — a commission kind, a standing order, or manual `nsg atlas refresh`? (Staleness quest `w-mnz8kxki` owns the scheduling question; layer 1 just needs to produce when invoked.)
=== w-mnz8kqb9-266e6bb9f95c ===
# Atlas layer 2: deterministic repository facts

## Goal

Design and ship the second layer of the Astrolabe atlas: deterministically computed structured facts about the repository (symbol index, dependency graph, test↔source map, public-API surface per package, recent-churn data) produced by non-LLM tooling. These facts back two consumption paths — (a) cheap MCP tools Astrolabe agents can call to verify ground truth mid-session without triggering a filesystem grind, and (b) selective structured snippets injected alongside the prose preamble when a commission specifically benefits from exact-name precision (e.g. a refactor commission that needs the real list of call sites, not an LLM's paraphrase). Outcome is (a) a list of fact types and their shapes, (b) tool selections (tree-sitter? madge? ast-grep? custom?), (c) a query surface definition, and (d) a decision on prose-alongside-structured preamble injection.

## Status

open — just opened 2026-04-14 as the second of three implementation-layer subquests under `w-mnyx6ajh`. Sequenced *after* layer 1 (`w-mnz8kj86`) per parent-quest ordering — layer 1 ships the turn-killer preamble; layer 2 adds the precision/fact-check layer on top. Can start design work in parallel with layer 1 implementation, but shouldn't ship until layer 1 has proven that preamble-shaped consumption actually moves the cost needle.

## Next Steps

1. **Enumerate the fact types we need.** Start from the questions Astrolabe's reader currently burns turns answering and back out what structured data would short-circuit each one. Seed list to iterate on:
   - **Symbol index**: "does this function exist, in which file, with what signature?"
   - **Exports per file**: "what's the public API of this module?"
   - **Import graph**: "who depends on this file? what does this file depend on?"
   - **Test↔source map**: "which tests cover this source file?"
   - **Package public-API surface**: "what does this package expose to consumers, not just internally?"
   - **Recent churn**: "which files have changed in the last N commits / are commonly modified together?"
   - **File-tree metadata**: "what's the shape of this directory, how big are the files, what are the types?"
   - **Build/config fingerprint**: "what's the tsconfig, lint config, test runner, formatter?"
2. **Pick query shapes per fact type.** For each fact, what question does a consumer ask?
   - Exact lookup (`atlas-exports <file>` → list of symbols)
   - Reverse lookup (`atlas-importers <file>` → list of files importing it)
   - Filtered traversal (`atlas-deps <package> --transitive` → dep closure)
   - Fuzzy/prefix search (`atlas-find-symbol <pattern>` → ranked matches)
   - Structural aggregation (`atlas-package-api <package>` → exported surface)
   - Differential (`atlas-churn --since <sha>` → changed files list)
3. **Select tooling per fact type.** This is where the real decisions live. Candidates to evaluate:
   - **tree-sitter** — cross-language parser, strong for symbol extraction and AST walks. Good match for the framework (TypeScript) and plugin code. Well-maintained grammars.

# Atlas layer 3: combatting staleness

## Goal

Decide and ship the staleness model for the Astrolabe atlas: how we detect drift, when we trigger refreshes, and how expensive refresh costs get amortized or avoided. Covers incremental structural-fact updates on file change, periodic full prose re-maps, git-SHA-based drift detection, and — most importantly — the design where "a fresh atlas map for workspace X" becomes a **declared engine precondition** that the multi-rig scheduler can satisfy by auto-prepending a mapper rig before the planning rig runs. This is where the atlas stops being a manually-maintained artifact and becomes a scheduled dependency of planning work. Outcome is (a) a drift-detection strategy (what counts as "stale enough to matter"), (b) a refresh policy per atlas layer (prose layer and deterministic layer refresh on different cadences), (c) a design for engine-declared atlas preconditions that couples into the multi-rig template-generation work, and (d) a clear story for how this interacts with the workspace model.

## Status

open — just opened 2026-04-14 as the third of three implementation-layer subquests under `w-mnyx6ajh`. Sequenced *last* per parent-quest ordering: staleness is only a problem once there's a valuable artifact to keep fresh, which requires layers 1 and 2 to have shipped and proven value. But the design work can start in parallel — especially the "engine-declared precondition" piece, because it couples tightly into the multi-rig refactor quest cluster and may influence decisions that get made there.

## Next Steps

1. **Pick a drift-detection primitive.** The easy wins first:
   - **Git SHA tracking**: store the SHA the atlas was built against; staleness = "any file in scope has changed since that SHA." Cheap (`git diff --name-only <sha>..HEAD`), correct, no false positives from wall-clock.
   - **File content hash**: per-file content hash stored alongside each layer-2 fact; invalidate on hash mismatch. More granular than SHA tracking, more metadata to maintain.
   - **Wall-clock TTL**: GSD's approach. Bad — false-positive on overnight, false-negative on active edits. Document as "don't do this."
   - **Change-coverage ratio**: "atlas is stale if >N% of in-scope files have changed since build." Tunable, useful as a refresh-*trigger* heuristic on top of exact SHA/hash tracking.
   Decide which combinations we need. My lean: SHA tracking as the base primitive, file-hash for per-fact invalidation in layer 2, change-coverage as the refresh trigger threshold.
2. **Define refresh policies per layer.** Layer 1 and layer 2 have different refresh cost profiles and should refresh on different cadences:
   - **Layer 2 (deterministic)**: near-zero cost per refresh. Can rebuild on every commit, every file save, every commission kickoff, whatever's convenient. The expensive question is *where* to trigger it (file watcher? git post-commit hook? engine precondition? commissioned rig?) not *whether* it's affordable.
   - **Layer 1 (prose)**: expensive per refresh ($5–10 for a full mapping run at GSD scale). Needs amortization across many commissions. Refresh only when interpretive drift is likely — not just "a file changed" but "enough files changed, or architecturally-important files changed, that the narrative is probably out of date." This is where change-coverage thresholds and possibly file-importance weights come in.
3. **Design the engine-precondition mechanism.** This is the load-bearing piece. Sketch:
   - An Astrolabe planning engine declares a *dependency* on "a fresh atlas for workspace X."
   - The scheduler checks atlas freshness before dispatching the planning engine.
   - If fresh: proceed.
   - If stale: the scheduler auto-prepends a mapper rig (or a smaller refresh rig for incremental updates) to the writ's rig sequence. The planning engine waits for the mapper to complete.
   - Atlas freshness is re-checked after the mapper completes; the planning engine then dispatches against the now-fresh atlas.
   - This is essentially "backward chaining on precondition satisfaction," which is one of the exact shapes the multi-rig template-generation quest (`w-mnsxawnd`) is already considering.
4. **Couple into the multi-rig quest cluster.** Read and engage with:
   - `w-mnsxawnd-5f868b6491d9` — *Rig template generation beyond 'one template per writ type'* — this is where the "engine declares a precondition, scheduler synthesizes a rig shape that satisfies it" primitive lives or should live. Atlas-freshness-as-precondition is a concrete use case that can inform that quest's design.
=== w-mnyp279g-f565be10b053 ===
# Tool-server MCP 500s drop mid-session on baseline astrolabe reader

## Goal

Find the root cause of a recurring failure mode in which a baseline astrolabe reader session starts with the MCP tool server healthy, successfully makes multiple MCP tool calls, and then mid-session every `inventory-write` (and other astrolabe tool) call starts returning HTTP 500 for the remainder of the session. The session's last-ditch retry loop burns several dollars and exits 0 without a usable inventory. We want to know *why the tool server stops responding mid-session*, not just defend against the symptom — this is distinct from the engine-precondition quest (`w-mny2ltvy`), which addresses the case where the tool is never reachable at start.

## Status

active — opened 2026-04-14 after the *second* recurrence on the same writ. Two sample rigs captured as evidence. Hypotheses are explicitly untested; this is the investigation bookmark.

## Next Steps

1. **Stand up a side-by-side transcript diff** of the two failed reader sessions. Look at tool-call history ordered by timestamp — when does the first 500 appear, what tool, what was the last successful call before it, how many tools had been called, what was the cumulative byte volume of tool *arguments* and *results*.
2. **Check the guild daemon log** (`/workspace/vibers/.nexus/logs/`) for anything interesting around the failure timestamps: `2026-04-14T13:40-13:48` (rig-mnynxqnu session `ses-mnynxr8e`) and the earlier `rig-mny49p49` window. Look specifically for SSE disconnects, tool-server restarts, Node unhandled rejections, EMFILE/ECONNRESET, proxy errors from the babysitter.
3. **Inspect the tool-server → babysitter transport.** The babysitter runs `createProxyMcpHttpServer()` (claude-code/src/babysitter.ts ~L238-353) which speaks SSE and forwards to `guildToolUrl`. Is the SSE channel dying (from either side) after N requests or T seconds? Does the proxy try to reconnect? Does a dead SSE channel return 500 to the model while the tool server is actually fine?
4. **Reproduce.** Pick the baseline reader template, give it the MCP-preconditions brief again, and run it in isolation under logging. If it reproduces reliably → bisect.
5. **Decide whether to add defenses** (retry on 500, proxy reconnect, tool-server client pool sizing) vs fixing the root cause alone.

## Context

### The failure shape

A session runs the astrolabe reader engine. Early in the session, MCP tool calls succeed — `plan-show`, possibly one or more `inventory-write` calls. Then, at some point partway through, *every* subsequent MCP tool call returns HTTP 500. The session's model-side retry logic retries repeatedly, burning tokens and wall-clock, and eventually gives up and narrates its own failure in prose. The session process exits 0 (no crash, no thrown exception) but the plan row has no inventory written, so the downstream `inventory-check` engine fails and the rig cascades to `failed`.

Crucially: **the early successful tool calls prove the tool server was reachable at start.** This is not a startup/precondition failure — the transport came up cleanly, did real work, and *then* died.

### Sample rigs

Both failures happened on the same writ (`w-mnynxpwd-17535f30f61a` — `[EXP] Engine-level MCP tool precondition checks`), baseline (`brief`) variant, in the astrolabe reader stage. The writ body is notably "meta" — a brief *about* MCP tool failures, citing a prior incident.

Here's the shape of where we are on astrolabe cost + GSD-intel adoption.

## The quest tree

```
w-mnt3t5h8  Astrolabe efficiency: reader/full-inventory cost reduction    [active]
└── w-mnyx6ajh  GSD-intel-style atlas for Astrolabe reader                 [active]
    ├── w-mnz8kj86  Atlas layer 1: prose preamble (LLM mapper)             [open, ships 1st]
    ├── w-mnz8kqb9  Atlas layer 2: deterministic repo facts                [open, ships 2nd]
    └── w-mnz8kxki  Atlas layer 3: combatting staleness                    [open, ships 3rd]

w-mnyp279g  Tool-server MCP 500s drop mid-session on baseline reader      [active, sibling]
```

The tool-server-500s quest isn't formally parented to the cost quest, but it was spun out of it and the fate of the baseline variant in the A/B turns on it, so it lives in the same neighborhood.

## Per-quest status, briefly

| Quest | Status in a couple words |
|---|---|
| **w-mnt3t5h8** Astrolabe efficiency (parent) | A/B running — 6 rigs parked at `decision-review`. First-pass numbers: **SSR hypothesis looks broken** (not hitting the 10–20× reader reduction); **MRA looks like the winner** on cost + wall-clock. Quality comparison still pending — that's the gate. |
| **w-mnyx6ajh** GSD-intel atlas (pitch) | Scoped and decomposed. GSD source teardown done (`2026-04-14-gsd-intel-teardown.md`), three-axes framing settled, split into three layer subquests in strict 1→2→3 order. Parent is now scaffolding. **Waiting on parent A/B quality gate** before layer 1 kicks off. |
| **w-mnz8kj86** Layer 1 — prose preamble | Freshly opened. This is the "turn-killer" — prose docs from an LLM mapper rig, injected as preamble, selected by brief-type→subset map. Ports ~4 GSD templates (STACK/ARCHITECTURE/STRUCTURE/CONVENTIONS), adds MODULE-MAP. Blocked on the A/B gate (consumer is either MRA or baseline). |
| **w-mnz8kqb9** Layer 2 — deterministic facts | Freshly opened. Structured facts (symbol index, import graph, test↔source, public-API surfaces, churn) via non-LLM tooling; consumed as MCP tools + preamble snippets. Design can run in parallel with layer 1; ships after. Tooling choice (tree-sitter/madge/ast-grep) is the main open call. |
| **w-mnz8kxki** Layer 3 — staleness | Freshly opened. Git-SHA drift detection + per-layer refresh policies; the load-bearing piece is **"fresh atlas as a declared engine precondition"** — couples directly into the multi-rig quest cluster (`w-mnsxawnd`, `w-mnsxbn8m`). Design starts in parallel to avoid foreclosure there. |
| **w-mnyp279g** Tool-server 500s mid-session | Investigation bookmark. Two recurrences on the same writ, both baseline variant, reader stage. Hypotheses captured (SSE proxy dying? babysitter transport?) but explicitly untested. Material to the A/B: if baseline stays fragile here, it's another thumb on the scale for MRA (which ran clean on identical writ bodies). |

## The shape in one paragraph

The A/B is sitting at `decision-review` waiting on quality comparison. Cost/timing already says SSR underdelivered and MRA is the cost/latency winner. **The atlas is the bet that comes after this:** we found the GSD `/gsd-intel` pattern, decided to port it, decomposed it into three layers that ship in order (prose-preamble → deterministic-facts → staleness/scheduling), and are holding layer 1 until the A/B quality gate clears — because layer 1's consumer is either MRA or the baseline reader depending on how that lands. Meanwhile, the tool-server 500s quest is the dangling fragility question that could either rehabilitate the baseline or push MRA further ahead.

**Next decision point:** quality comparison on the 6 parked rigs. That unblocks everything downstream.

39

56	
57	**Per-brief totals (reader + analyst combined):**
58	
59	| Brief | baseline | SSR | MRA |
60	|---|---|---|---|
61	| MCP-precond | $4.30 / 1040s ⚠️ | $4.62 / 700s | **$2.35 / 500s** |
62	| prompt-inj  | $2.31 / 460s    | $2.13 / 500s  | **$1.66 / 345s** |
63	
64	### Findings vs hypotheses
65	
66	**H1: Reader dominated by turn count (~25).** The profile said baseline reader ~25 turns mean. Current measurement: baseline prompt-inj reader ran **86 assistant turns / 60 tool calls**, baseline MCP-precond reader 84 turns / 55 tool calls. Either the profile's "turn" was counted differently (tool-call *pairs*, or some other unit) or the reader has gotten chattier. Either way, our mental model of "~25 turns" is wrong for this batch — the baseline reader is doing ~3× that. Worth reconciling the measurement before treating the 25-turn number as a baseline for future planning.
67	
68	**H2: SSR single-shot should be 10–20× cheaper than baseline reader.** **Not confirmed — and now we know why.**
69	- Prompt-inj: SSR reader $1.05 / 190s vs baseline reader $1.43 / 220s — maybe 25% cheaper, possibly within noise.
70	- MCP-precond: SSR reader $2.66 / 305s vs baseline $3.57 / 840s (inflated). Removing the retry tail, clean baseline likely ~$1.50–2.00, which would make SSR *comparable or worse*.
71	- SSR reader cache-read is still in the millions (3.25M on MCP-precond, 0.49M on prompt-inj) — not the shape of a 1–2 turn session.
72	
73	**Root cause (2026-04-14 transcript deep-dive): zero parallel tool calls across ALL 8 sessions in the experiment.** Every single assistant turn across baseline / SSR / MRA / both briefs emitted *exactly one* `tool_use` block, never two, never three. The SSR prompt explicitly demanded parallel batching (*"Issue ALL of these tool calls together in a single parallel batch — do not wait for results before issuing more reads"*) and set a hard "maximum 3 turns" budget. Actual: 79 turns on SSR prompt-inj, 107 on SSR MCP-precond, all serial. The model **narrates** parallelization in almost every text block (*"Let me read X, Y, Z in parallel"*, *"Now let me read the critical source files in parallel"*) and then emits a single tool_use block each time.
74	
75	| Session | Max tools/turn | Multi-tool turns | Total tool calls |
76	|---|---|---|---|
77	| baseline MCP reader (failed) | 1 | 0 | 55 |
78	| baseline prompt-inj reader | 1 | 0 | 60 |
79	| baseline prompt-inj analyst | 1 | 0 | 17 |
80	| SSR MCP reader+analyst | 1 | 0 | 66 |
81	| SSR prompt-inj reader+analyst | 1 | 0 | 55 |
82	| MRA MCP reader-analyst | 1 | 0 | 50 |
83	| MRA prompt-inj reader-analyst | 1 | 0 | 54 |
84	| baseline MCP analyst (post-recovery) | 1 | 0 | 16 |
85	
86	SSR's entire cost-reduction premise was "collapse N serial tool calls into 1 parallel-batch turn." **If the model cannot (or will not) emit multiple tool_use blocks in a single assistant message in this environment, SSR is definitionally impossible in its current form.** This explains the observation completely.
87	
88	### Hypotheses for why the model is 100% serial
89	
90	1. **Opus-specific behavior.** Guild runs `model: "opus"`. Opus may be trained/tuned to issue tool calls more conservatively and serially. A sonnet-backed SSR run would discriminate — cheap test, one rig. *(Untested — candidate next move if SSR is worth rescuing.)*
91	2. **Claude Code runtime serialization in Nexus's plugin.** *(RULED OUT 2026-04-14 via code review — see below.)*
92	3. **Claude CLI headless (`--print`) mode behavior or its built-in system prompt.** The claude CLI is spawned with `--print -`, `--output-format stream-json`, `--verbose`, `--setting-sources user`, `--dangerously-skip-permissions`, `--model opus`, `--system-prompt-file <path>`, `--mcp-config <path>`, `--strict-mcp-config`. Headless mode may have a different tool-loop or system prompt layering than interactive mode — possibly one that doesn't include the "emit parallel tool calls when possible" encouragement. This is the most likely remaining cause, but it lives in the claude CLI binary (outside this repo).
93	4. **Compound of the above.** Multiple small pressures that together make parallel batching essentially never happen.
94	
95	### Ruling out hypothesis 2 — claude-code plugin code review (2026-04-14)
96	
97	Reviewed `packages/plugins/claude-code/src/` (babysitter.ts, detached.ts, index.ts). **No smoking gun. The plugin is a faithful passthrough.**
98	
99	- **Stream-json parser preserves all content blocks.** `parseStreamJsonMessage` in `index.ts` (lines 155-170) pushes the entire raw assistant message to `acc.transcript` *before* per-block processing, and then iterates `for (const block of content)` over every block for chunk emission. If the claude CLI had emitted a multi-tool_use message, every tool_use block would appear in our captured transcripts. **The "0 multi-tool turns across 8 sessions" finding is therefore definitive: the claude CLI itself is emitting exactly one tool_use block per assistant message in this environment.**
100	- **MCP proxy is standard.** `createProxyMcpHttpServer` (`babysitter.ts` ~L274-388) uses the MCP SDK's `Server` class with an SSE transport; the `CallToolRequestSchema` handler (L305-326) is one-call-in/one-forward-out with no batching or serialization. Parallel tool calls from the model would arrive as independent MCP requests handled independently.
101	- **Claude CLI spawn args are standard.** Nothing disables parallelism: no `MAX_TOOL_USE`, no `DISABLE_PARALLEL`, no hidden env var. `grep -i` for `DISABLE_PARALLEL|MAX_TOOL|PARALLEL|concurrent|CLAUDE_CODE_|max_tokens` across the entire plugin source returns exactly one match — a SQLite WAL mode comment (unrelated).
102	- **`--setting-sources user`** limits which setting files are applied (no project-level `.claude/settings.json`, no working-directory CLAUDE.md). User-level settings and the CLI's built-in defaults still apply. This flag could theoretically matter *if* the "parallel tool calls" hint lives at the project-settings level, but that's speculation — we'd need Claude Code CLI source to verify.
103	
104	Conclusion: **Nexus is not serializing tool calls.** Whatever is happening, it's upstream of Nexus — in the claude CLI binary, its headless-mode system prompt layering, or the model's own behavior. Code review cannot progress further without reading Claude Code CLI source (not in this repo).
105	
106	### MRA wins anyway — and its win mechanism is different
107	
108	MRA doesn't depend on parallel tool calls. Its cost advantage comes from:
109	1. **No second session startup/bootstrap** after reader → analyst handoff.
110	2. **No cache reload** between stages — one conversation context covers both phases.
111	3. **The model decides naturally when to stop reading and start analyzing**, without an artificial stage boundary.
112	
113	MRA's per-turn shape is identical to baseline/SSR (1 tool call per turn), but it does fewer *total* turns (75 vs baseline's 86+29 = 115) and avoids the handoff overhead. **MRA is provisionally promotable on cost alone**, pending quality comparison.
114	
115	**H3: MRA ≈ baseline analyst alone.** **Partially confirmed — MRA wins on cost in both briefs, but it's not quite "analyst alone."**
116	- Prompt-inj: MRA $1.66 vs baseline analyst $0.88 — MRA is ~2× the pure analyst cost, because it's also doing the reading. But $0.65 cheaper than baseline total ($2.31), $0.47 cheaper than SSR total ($2.13).
117	- MCP-precond: MRA $2.35, cheapest even against a clean-baseline correction.
118	- MRA is also the **fastest wall-clock** in both briefs. No reader↔analyst handoff latency.
119	
120	**H4: Brief-shape effects.** Bigger brief (MCP-precond, ~25 KB) runs slower and more expensive across all variants, but the *ranking* between variants is consistent across briefs. No variant flipped ordering by brief. The tool-dropout failure on baseline/MCP-precond is *not* a brief-shape effect — it's a distinct tool-server pathology (see `w-mnyp279g`).
121	
122	### Headline picture
123	
124	- **MRA is the provisional winner** on both cost and wall-clock across both briefs, by a meaningful margin.
125	- **SSR is a disappointment** — not collapsing turns the way intervention A predicted. May be rescuable with a prompt fix, may not. **This is the open investigation:** read SSR transcripts and figure out why it's still multi-turn.
126	- **Baseline carries a tail-risk** on specific writ content (2/2 tool-dropout failures on the same writ, different runs). That's a reliability finding even if the cost numbers are within striking distance.
127	- **Quality hasn't been compared yet** — if SSR or MRA produced meaningfully worse plans, cost wins don't matter. That's the gate on any promotion decision.

130	
131	Side-by-side read of all four plan stages (inventory, scope, decisions, observations) across baseline / SSR / MRA for the prompt-injection brief. Plan writs: baseline `w-mnynxfhj-2b38ea890673`, SSR `w-mnyny4bg-28b1974bb033`, MRA `w-mnynyjub-a5398e15e8ea`.
132	
133	**Headline: no quality degradation on this brief across any variant.** All three converge on the same architectural core (scanner as standalone module, tool-handler layer is the scan site, scan result threaded via request types, flat const-array pattern table, per-pattern case sensitivity, windowed excerpts). Disagreements are at the edges — and that's where quality actually shows.

135	### Inventory depth

137	All three caught the `writ-post`-doesn't-exist, `commission-post.ts`-CLI-doesn't-exist, and tool-handler-caller-identity-gap findings. **MRA uniquely caught** the stale `nsg writ accept` CLI README; **SSR uniquely** included a numbered patron-text→prompt pipeline and a package dependency graph. **Baseline** has the most detailed trade-off narrative and inline signature reprints but is the most verbose.

184	
185	Side-by-side read of all four plan stages across baseline / SSR / MRA for the MCP-preconditions brief. Plan writs: baseline `w-mnynxpwd-17535f30f61a` ⚠ (recovery cell — reader salvaged via transcript extraction, analyst ran against restored inventory; flag when reporting), SSR `w-mnynxz7n-d658562985a6`, MRA `w-mnynycgm-2200291d4972`.

187	**This brief is substantially harder than prompt-injection** — more type-system coupling, more architectural layering, and a genuine unresolvable tension in the brief itself (brief says "extend EngineDesign" but the canonical engine `anima-session` is generic and can't meaningfully carry the declaration). The quality dispersion shows it.

190	
191	All three caught the `mcp-server.ts` nonexistent-file discrepancy and the `anima-session`-is-generic problem. Differences:

201	**D1 — where does `requiredMcpTools` live?**

203	| | Choice | Implication |
204	|---|---|---|
205	| **Baseline** | **`RigTemplateEngine` only** | Drops the brief's suggestion. "Extend EngineDesign" is wrong for generic designs; baseline just says so. No merge logic. |

252	### Verdict for MCP-preconditions brief

260	## Cross-brief synthesis (both briefs)
261	
262	| | Prompt-injection | MCP-preconditions |
263	|---|---|---|
264	| **Baseline** | Thorough, some filler, ducks `--strict`-when-`off` | **Sharpest architectural stances** (D1, D15), moderate coverage |
265	| **SSR** | Roughly on par, one weak sub-decision (combined title+body scan) | Solid but least distinctive, best observations catch |
266	| **MRA** | **Sharper decision substance** (D10 full-rescan, D6 extended-shape) | **Highest per-decision depth**, weaker on D1/D15 |
267	
268	**Pattern: MRA consistently covers *more questions* and makes *fewer filler decisions*, but trends toward "cover all the design space" rather than "commit to the sharpest position on load-bearing calls."** On the prompt-injection brief that works in MRA's favor (its D10 and D6 are real design wins). On the harder MCP-preconditions brief, MRA's breadth coexists with weaker load-bearing calls while baseline's comparative brevity conceals sharper underlying judgment.
269	
270	### Revised promotion verdict
271	
272	1. **Promote MRA on cost + wall-clock** — cost/speed case is unambiguous, quality case is at-least-equal across both briefs. No reason to keep paying baseline's latency.
273	2. **Add patron-review emphasis at `decision-review`** — MRA's "cover the design space" tendency means its plans benefit from patron sharpening on load-bearing decisions when briefs have architectural tensions. Baseline's D1/D15 calls are a data point for what that sharpening looks like.
274	3. **Kill SSR** — two briefs, no distinctive quality win, broken cost premise (serial-tool-calls finding), no rescue without upstream claude-CLI changes. Park the templates, stop running the A/B.
275	4. **n=1 caveat persists** — worth one more observation round post-promotion (not a full A/B, just continued MRA monitoring on production astrolabe) before closing the quest entirely.

302	- Astrolabe spend captured: **$107.35** across 51 sessions (22 reader, 17 analyst, 12 spec-writer) in the guild books.
303	- **Reader is 65% of astrolabe's spend** ($69.75). Mean $3.17/session, max $8.22, 5.2× spread.
304	- **Reader's cost is dominated by turn count, not load size.** Mean ~25 turns per session, each re-reading its accumulated ~107 k-token cache. The cheapest run loaded 66 k and took 24 turns; the most expensive loaded 118 k and took 26 turns but cost 5× more — turn-count multiplier dominates.
305	- Reader produces a **well-bounded, structured output** (~17 k tokens, consistent sections every time). The agent already knows the deliverable shape — it's spending 25 turns getting there via filesystem poking.
306	- Conversation-cache-sharing between reader and downstream saves only ~27% on downstream cost, much less than expected. Downstream stages still load ~1.3 M cache-read of their own material. Suggests reader may be partially redundant with analyst.
307	- 2/22 reader sessions exit-failed at ~$2.40 each. One session silently ran in a degraded mode because its output tools (plan-show, inventory-write) weren't available — it wrote a file to the worktree and still billed $2.26. Separate observability gap worth a dedicated quest.
308	
309	**Intervention ranking from the profile:**
310	
311	- **A. Single-shot reader prompt** (highest leverage) — collapse ~25 turns → 1–2. Estimated 10–20× cost reduction. Dispatched as a commission.
312	- **B. Merge reader into analyst** — removes 65% of spend; bigger design risk but bigger upside if it holds quality. Dispatched as a commission.
313	- **C. Shrink per-turn cache-read** — 20–40% savings; order of magnitude less leverage than A. Not pursued yet.
314	- **D. Fail-fast on tool-availability** — hygiene/correctness, not cost. Candidate for its own quest.
315	- **E. Retry failed readers** — not pursuing; root-cause fix via A/B addresses it.
316	
317	A and B were dispatched concurrently as experimental secondary rig templates so production astrolabe (`brief` → `planning`) stays untouched while we A/B test. Each experimental variant gets its own writ type so commissions route to the right template without any global flag.
318	
319	**Related earlier thinking:** the Unified instrument context for cache efficiency quest attacks a similar shape of problem on the instrument side (shared prefix, many suffixes). Cost-tracking work on Coinmaster/Purse/Tithe is the measurement side — once that exists, profiling becomes continuous rather than one-off.
320	
321	## References
322	
323	- **`experiments/data/2026-04-10-astrolabe-profile.md`** — full profile findings (51-session cohort, intervention ranking, recommended experimental design).
324	- `/workspace/nexus/packages/plugins/astrolabe/src/astrolabe.ts` — framework source; the `planningTemplate` rig template and `rigTemplateMappings` block are where the experimental variants hook in.
325	- `/workspace/nexus/packages/plugins/astrolabe/src/engines/` — existing engine implementations.
326	- Commission briefs: `.scratch/brief-astrolabe-ssr-template.md` (w-mnt966d8), `.scratch/brief-astrolabe-mra-template.md` (w-mnt968x4).
327	- Related quest: **Unified instrument context for cache efficiency** — prompt-prefix sharing trick for instrument runs; may generalize to astrolabe stages.
328	- Related quest: **Cost tracking & token budget allocation (Coinmaster/Purse/Tithe)** `w-mnszznt5-66d0ec7464bc`.
329	- Related quest: **Engine completion should be predicated on tool-call trace, not session exit** `w-mnt3r8al-b36b42b253b2`.
330	- Guild vocabulary: `docs/future/guild-vocabulary.md`.
331	
332	## Notes
333	
334	- (2026-04-10) Sean: "increase astrolabe efficiency. it is very slow and costly right now, and i think the reader/full inventory is a big part of that"
335	- (2026-04-10) Profile confirmed Sean's instinct — reader is the dominant cost, but the surprising finding is that the dominance comes from *turn count* not *load size*. This reframes the intervention space: caching/scoping wins much less than prompt restructuring.
336	- (2026-04-10) Observability gap flagged but not yet captured as its own quest: reader silently degrades when output tools aren't available. Create follow-up quest if the single-shot work doesn't incidentally address it.
337
